Created: 2026-03-30T03:48:24.000Z
Metrics: {"retweet_count":8,"reply_count":2,"like_count":35,"quote_count":1,"bookmark_count":69,"impression_count":3213}
Article content: {"plain_text":"Claude Code has been shipping at a pace that is honestly hard to keep up with. A major release roughly every two weeks since January. New features, new tools, new ways to work. If you took two weeks off, you missed more than you think.
This is the everything guide. Every major feature is now live in Claude Code as of March 2026. How to set each one up, when to use what, and the operator patterns that separate people who think this tool is "cool" from people who have rebuilt how they ship around it.
Bookmark this. Come back to it. Share it with your team. This is the reference I wish existed when I started.
The models: which one, when
Opus 4.6 is the ceiling. 1M token context window (GA since March 13, no price multiplier). 5/25 per million tokens. 128K max output. Use it for complex analysis, codebase refactoring, deep research, and anything where quality matters more than cost. Do not use it for tasks you run more than a few times a day — a heavy Opus workflow burns $50-100/day.
Sonnet 4.6 is the default. 1M context (GA). 3/15 per million tokens. 30-50% faster than Sonnet 4.5. Early testers preferred it over the previous flagship Opus 4.5 in 59% of cases. Use it for everyday coding, agent workflows, and anything where speed matters.
Haiku 4.5 is the cheap worker. Use it for subagents that read files, grep code, and do simple lookups. Important caveat: Haiku has limited prompt injection protection. Do not process untrusted input through it.
The move here: Set your lead agent to Sonnet. Set exploration/review subagents to Haiku. Escalate to Opus only when Sonnet's output is not good enough. This alone can cut your API costs by 60%.
CLAUDE.md: the file that changes everything
CLAUDE.md is the most important file in your project. Claude reads it at session start and follows it for every interaction. Whatever you write, Claude does.
What belongs in it:
build, test, lint commands (the exact commands, not descriptions)
architecture overview (framework, language, folder structure — 5 lines max)
conventions (naming, error handling, import patterns)
gotchas (the non-obvious things Claude gets wrong without being told)
What does not belong: Documentation, theory, rationale, anything over 200 lines. Long CLAUDE.md files degrade instruction adherence.
The move here: Run /init in your project. Claude generates a starter CLAUDE.md. Then edit it down to essentials. Add one gotcha every session. The file compounds — by session 100, Claude knows everything non-obvious about your codebase.
Skills: the auto-invoking workflows
Skills are markdown files that Claude loads automatically when the task matches. You do not type a command. Claude reads the skill description and activates it when relevant.
Every skill lives in .claude/skills/skill-name/SKILL.md. The frontmatter defines when it fires:
 
the move here: Install superpowers (git clone https://github.com/obra/superpowers ~/.claude/skills/superpowers). It adds 20+ battle-tested skills covering TDD, debugging, and plan-to-execute workflows. 96K stars for a reason.
Custom commands: your slash shortcuts
Every markdown file in .claude/commands/ becomes a slash command. review.mdcreates /project:review. fix-issue.md creates /project:fix-issue.
Commands support arguments via $ARGUMENTS and shell execution via !`command`backticks. This means a command can inject live git diffs, issue details, or test output directly into Claude's context.
the move here: Create .claude/commands/review.md that runs git diff main...HEAD and asks Claude to review the changes. You will use this 10 times a day.
Agents: specialized subagent personas
Agents in .claude/agents/ are markdown files with their own system prompt, tool restrictions, and model preferences. Claude spawns them in isolated context windows for focused tasks.
 
The move here: Create a code-reviewer.md agent on Sonnet and a code-explorer.md agent on Haiku. The explorer searches cheaply. The reviewer evaluates carefully. Your main session stays clean.
Agent teams: parallel execution
Agent teams let you coordinate multiple Claude Code sessions working together. One session leads, others execute in parallel, each in its own context window.
Enable with CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1. Teammates spawn in 20-30 seconds. A 3-teammate team uses roughly 3-4x the tokens but cuts wall-clock time by 3-5x on parallelizable work.
When to use: Research and review (multiple aspects simultaneously), new modules, debugging with competing hypotheses, cross-layer coordination (frontend + backend + tests).
When not to use: Sequential tasks, same-file edits, and working with many dependencies. Use subagents instead.
The move here: Try agent teams on your next large PR review. One teammate reviews frontend, one reviews backend, and one reviews tests. The lead synthesizes findings.
Hooks: event-driven automation
Hooks are shell commands or prompts that fire on specific events: PreToolUse, PostToolUse, SessionStart, SessionEnd, UserPromptSubmit, PreCompact, and Stop.
Configure in .claude/settings.json:
 
The move here: Add a PostToolUse hook that runs your linter after every file write. Instant feedback. No manual step.
MCP servers: external tool access
MCP (Model Context Protocol) gives Claude access to external services — databases, APIs, browsers, search engines. Connect via Claude MCP add.
The 3 MCP servers worth installing today:
Context7 — injects up-to-date library docs. Add "use context7" to your prompt, and Claude gets current docs instead of hallucinated APIs.
Playwright — browser automation. Claude can click, fill forms, take screenshots, and verify that the UI it built actually works.
Tavily — search engine built for agents. Clean structured data, not blue links.
The move here: Run Claude MCP add context7. The next time Claude hallucinates an API method, it will check the docs first.
Git worktrees: isolated parallel work
claude --worktree launches a session in a temporary git worktree — an isolated copy of your repo. The agent can make changes without touching your working branch. If the changes are good, merge them. If not, the worktree gets cleaned up automatically.
The move here: Use worktrees for any experimental task where you want Claude to try something without risking your current work.
Auto-memory: Claude remembers across sessions
Claude Code automatically saves observations to ~/.claude/projects/ — commands it discovers, patterns it observes, and architecture insights. These persist across sessions. Browse and edit with /memory.
The move here: After a long debugging session, check /memory. Claude probably captured the fix. That knowledge is now permanent.
The permission model
settings.json controls what Claude can and cannot do. allow lists commands that run without confirmation. deny blocks commands entirely. Everything else prompts for approval.
 
The move here: Start permissive. Tighten when you discover edge cases. The permission model is your safety net, not your productivity blocker.
The compound effect
The engineers getting the most from Claude Code are not the ones with the cleverest prompts. They are the ones who built systems around the tool: CLAUDE.md files that compound, skills that fire automatically, agents that specialize, hooks that automate, and permissions that protect.
Each of these features is simple on its own. Together, they create an operating system for AI-native development. The gap between a default Claude Code session and a configured one is the gap between a new hire and a teammate who has worked with you for a year.
The tool is the same for everyone. The configuration is not.
https://x.com/nyk_builderz/status/2030904887186514336?s=20
What is the one Claude Code feature from this guide you are going to set up today?

A major release roughly every two weeks since January. New features, new tools, new ways to work. If you took two weeks","title":"Everything Claude Code can do in 2026 (and how to actually use it)"}
